# Pixel-Art Player Ship — Design

## Goal

Replace the player's plain white circle (`draw_ball`, `Canvas.arc`) with a detailed pixel-art ship sprite, styled after a reference silhouette (`docs/superpowers/specs/assets/player-ship2.png` — a vertical hull with twin mast prongs, two upper towers merging into one body, a mid-body vent notch, and a forked twin-leg base with foot flares). Enemy ships stay as their current triangle — this is scoped to the player ship only.

## Approach: extracted pixel data, procedural fill_rect, pre-rendered once

The reference PNG (1024×1536) turned out to have smoothly anti-aliased shading, not a literal small native pixel grid — there's no single "true" resolution to losslessly extract. Instead of hand-authoring an approximation (which took many failed iterations), the actual pixel data below was **extracted mathematically**: the source image was loaded into a canvas, downsampled to a 64×96 grid by averaging every source pixel within each cell's region, and each cell's averaged luminance/alpha was classified into a 5-value palette. This is a precise, reproducible derivation of the source art, not a guess.

Rendering stays fully procedural (`Canvas.fill_rect`, no image loading) to match this codebase's existing convention (zero external image assets anywhere — starfield, nebula, ships, asteroids are all drawn from primitives). The extracted grid is the *data*; the *rendering mechanism* is the same kind of procedural drawing everything else in this game already uses.

**Performance:** at 64×96 resolution, drawing every non-background cell as an individual `fill_rect` every frame is unnecessary work — consecutive same-tone cells in a row are merged into one wider `fill_rect` (typically ~15-25 runs per row instead of up to 64 individual cells), and even that reduced set is drawn only **once**, at boot, into an offscreen canvas (the exact pattern already established for the WebGL nebula's offscreen canvas in `fx_init()`). Every frame, drawing the ship is just one `Canvas.draw_node` call — translate, rotate, blit.

## Data model

`Fx` gains one field: `player_sprite : Option(Node)` — the offscreen canvas holding the pre-rendered sprite (`Option` because `Canvas.get_context` is itself `Option`-returning, same defensive pattern as `Fx.gl`; in practice a freshly created canvas always succeeds).

`fx_init()` creates a detached `<canvas>` via `Dom.create("canvas")`, sized to the sprite's final in-game footprint (32×48 world units — 64:96 aspect preserved, a clearly visible but not absurd size relative to existing entities), gets its 2D context, and — if `Some` — draws the full pixel grid onto it once via a new `draw_player_sprite_pixels(ctx)` function. That function computes each cell's pixel-rounded rect bounds directly at the 32×48 target scale (same rounding technique used to fix the visual-companion mockup's earlier sub-pixel gap-line bug: round both edges to the nearest whole pixel so adjacent cells share exact boundaries, no seams).

## Rotation

The player ball currently has no heading concept — `draw_ball` just draws a circle at `(ball_x, ball_y)`, no rotation. The sprite's "nose" is at the top of its local pixel grid (row 0), not at local +X — the existing rotation convention (established by `ship_heading`/enemy ships) assumes heading 0 = nose pointing +X. So compositing needs a fixed `+π/2` offset added to whatever heading is computed, before calling `Canvas.rotate`.

A new `ball_heading(game) : Float` mirrors `ship_heading`'s two cases:
- `Flying(vx, vy)` → `Math.atan2(vy, vx)`
- `Orbiting(idx, ring, angle)` → `angle - halfPi` (same `-orbit_dir(idx) * halfPi` shape `ship_heading`'s `ShipOrbiting` case uses — `orbit_dir` is always `1.0` today, so this simplifies to a flat `angle - halfPi`, but written the same way for consistency in case that ever changes)

`draw_ball` becomes: match `fx.player_sprite`; on `Some(node)`, `Canvas.save`/`translate(ball_x, ball_y)`/`rotate(ball_heading(game) + halfPi)`/`draw_node(node, -16.0, -24.0)` (centering: half of 32×48)/`restore`; on `None`, fall back to the existing circle draw (defensive — matches how the nebula degrades gracefully when `fx.gl` is `None`, and costs nothing extra since the circle-drawing code doesn't need to change, just gets moved into the `None` arm). The shield ring (drawn separately, already circle-based) is unaffected — it still draws around `(ball_x, ball_y)` regardless of ship rotation.

## Extracted pixel grid (64 cols × 96 rows)

Palette: `0` = background (skip), `1` = `#e2e2e2` (light hull), `2` = `#333333` (dark), `3` = `#ffffff` (white highlight), `4` = `#9a9a9a` (mid gray).


(Leading/trailing all-zero rows can be trimmed by the implementer — they contribute nothing to the rendered sprite. Source PNG kept at `docs/superpowers/specs/assets/player-ship2.png` for reference/future re-extraction.)

## Testing approach

No automated test coverage — this is a pure rendering change with no gameplay-logic surface (no new `Game` fields, no new collision/scoring behavior), verified by browser inspection matching how the WebGL nebula and every other visual-only change in this game has been verified. Existing `test_core.march`/`test_combat.march` suites are unaffected (nothing about ball position/physics/collision changes, only how it's drawn).
